claim_text: The benchmark experiments run on an Intel Core i9-12900KS CPU, 32 GB RAM, an NVIDIA RTX 3090 Ti GPU, and Ubuntu 22.04 software.
evidence_text: Experimental Setup All experiments are conducted on a desktop PC with an Intel Core i9-12900KS CPU, 32 GB RAM, and an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 Ti GPU, running in a Docker container with Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2 Humble, and Autoware.

claim_text: The solver comparison shows second-order solving at N=3 matches first-order accuracy at N=5 while reducing required denoising steps.
evidence_text: The vertical arrow shows the solver order benefit: at the same latency, second-order solving at N=3 matches the accuracy of first-order at N=5, effectively halving the required steps.

claim_text: HiDrive states that benchmark saturation reflects limited case diversity, object variety, capability breadth, and invalidates earlier evaluation leaderboards.
evidence_text: This satura- tion does not mean that the problem has been solved; instead, it reveals that current benchmarks remain limited in scenario diversity, object variety, and the breadth of driving capabilities they evaluate.

claim_text: RoboDriveVLM-TTA records the best overall AvgL2-Prompt and AvgCol-Prompt results while maintaining relatively low MCL2 and MCC values under prompt corruption.
evidence_text: In particular, RoboDriveVLM-TTA achieves the best overall performance in AvgL2-Prompt (0.474), AvgCol-Prompt (0.22), and maintains relatively low MCL2/MCC values even under prompt corruption.

claim_text: NAVSIM reports challenging evaluation scenarios where the constant-velocity agent scores 95% and the human expert scores 22% in testing.
evidence_text: The resulting scenarios are challenging, which is underlined by the score of the constant velocity agent dropping to 22%, whereas the human expert achieves a score of 95%.

claim_text: DriveE2E introduces a closed-loop evaluation framework that integrates recorded traffic cases into CARLA through infrastructure cooperation for end-to-end autonomy evaluation.
evidence_text: To address these limitations, we introduce a simple yet challenging closed-loop evaluation framework that closely integrates real-world driving scenarios into the CARLA simulator with infrastructure cooperation.

claim_text: The authors introduce HABIT as a high-fidelity simulation benchmark for representing realistic human behavior in autonomous-system evaluation within CARLA studies.
evidence_text: Conclusion and future work In this work, we introduced HABIT (Human Action Bench- mark for Interactive Traffic), a high-fidelity simulation benchmark designed to address the critical limitations of current autonomous driving (AD) simulations in repre- senting realistic human behavior.

claim_text: The V-Max experiments implement LQ, LQH, MTR, and Wayformer encoder architectures with the Flax library as evaluated model components.
evidence_text: We implemented the following encoder architectures with the Flax library (Heek et al., 2024): (1) Latent-query (LQ): inspired from (Jaegle et al., 2021), (2) Latent-query hierarchical (LQH) (Bronstein et al., 2022); (3) Motion Transformer (MTR) (Shi et al., 2024), (4) Wayformer (Nayakanti et al., 2023).

claim_text: The V-Max paper describes imitation learning as the dominant paradigm while noting distribution shift and imitation gaps as key limitations.
evidence_text: Imitation Learning (IL) remains the dominant paradigm, benefiting from large-scale human demonstration datasets, but it suffers from inherent limitations such as distri- bution shift and imitation gaps.

evidence_text: Our results show that, despite the lengthy and costly training, state-of-the-art MARL algorithms rarely outperformed humans.

[3] These tasks demand sophisticated technical capabilities and domain expertise.

claim_text: The reward design sets the highest value at zero to stabilize Q-function learning and accelerate training after successful swing-ups.
